speaker: interviewer
text: January 21, 1996. Survivor, Kaz Wolff-Zdzienicki. Interviewer, Padigail Meskin. City of Byrne Village, Natal. Country, South Africa. Language, English. Kaz, can we just go back to some of the facts about-- that took place in the-- while you were involved in the war crimes? Were-- was the organization, the administration, et cetera, would you consider to be well-organized?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The way that they set the organization up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I must say it was very organized, was efficient. Of course, we had good stuff there, technical stuff. We had a company of women secretaries who could collect in shorthand, you know, during the interrogation. So it was very organized.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had, for example-- they have been clever. Among the interrogation officers, among the interpreter, was plenty of German or Austrian Jews who spoke fluently German. And they left Germany just after Hitler came to power. So they had about 15 years or 10 years, minimum, to learn proper English, with including American slang. So it was a big help, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was, of course, for example, some of the foreign countries, like France, sent people who were in the underground, in the resistance or in the intelligence. I was working with one French captain like that. They had been people not new to this kind of problems.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was clever, you know? Because very often, a young American officer could be, I would say, put in a completely wrong direction with his opinion about the war criminal whom he interrogated, you know? Because he was not enough clever, he accepted this, what he was told as the truth. It was a lie. And so it was a question. It was good organized, generally good. Well, one thing which I personally blame the American, this is for escaping by Bogar, you know, the lack of supervision. But what you can do? The army who was on the front in '44, '45, was sent back home. And then arrived youngster, completely unaware of what mean military discipline, orders, and this and that. And they had been still treating their job like playing with baseball in a, in a college, you know? And they were, they were not serious. So this happened sometime. Did you have regular time off? Were you given or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You see, I, in my job I had my time off, of course. I had some paperwork to do in the office, you see, to read the interrogation from the night, preparing myself for the next interrogation with the same person, of course. But some periods were a little, I would say, difficult. And I was under pressure when I had to interrogate somebody couple of day, day after day, day after day-- sorry-- night after night. But then I had, for example, 10 days or two weeks off, in this sense, that I had to prepare interrogation and read the papers, which I had to do in the future about him, some deposition. Because some, some files arrived already with a, a witness report, you know, with interrogation, previous interrogation. So one-- I should not ask twice the same question when the question is properly answered, you know, so to find the holes in between what's going on to make it clear, clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was there-- did you have any kind of social life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not really, you see. Because I personally, I don't mix with the-- haven't mixed with the social life with a German. I had couple of Polish chaps working with me in the interrogation office. I had couple of-- I had a friend who was-- I can remember his name even now, come back. It was Anje Grienski.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was in the security. He was a-- in the provost marshal office, you know? So we were close together. There was another. My friend from a concentration camp, from Auschwitz, was Piler. His father was a architect in, in Poland. And his mother came from Norway. So he was blonde with blue eyes like his mother, you know? He had a very, very low number in Auschwitz, 900-something. He arrived in 1940, he was arrested and to the end. Was he also in the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in there, yeah. And then he was as well there. So we keep together a little bit, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you'd go out sometimes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were out sometime. The officers' casino was a good food and cheap drinks. And we had a theater coming from United States with plenty of beautiful girls and this. So, so we had always our time for a little bit of fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, did you have time to-- did you meet or mix with the Germans at all? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Not at-- you [? had-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I had enough German when I talk to them--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the interrogations.

speaker: interviewee
text: In my, in my duty.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. With your observation of the prosecuting counsels, you were mainly involved, of course, with American prosecuting counsel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the defense counsels, were they also American?

speaker: interviewee
text: French counsel, yes, I had some contact with them. I had some, some contact with the Polish prosecution who was there. From time to time, they came. And you see, I had a tie with them to try to give them extradite for the extradition of some people to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you find that the way the prosecution was organized was very efficient?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was well done?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any particular problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, generally, yes. Generally, yes. Well, except some-- you know, for example, Ilse Koch-- Ilse Koch, according to this what I heard, she married an American sergeant afterwards and went to United States. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, I don't know if she is still in United States. Or maybe she is still alive. Or what's happened to her. But this was a case, a very, I would say, a big fan when I was there still, that she is involved with a American sergeant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the defense counsels, those defending the German, the Nazis, were they Germans, or were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Mostly American officers from American attorneys, lawyer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mainly American?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they was some of them, but only a very few, very few were allowed. More, I would say, very few wanted do this job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which I understand very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: The trials you were involved with, they were all part of the actual Nuremberg trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they were-- they were a different--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not Nuremberg-- you see, Nuremberg court was a different story. And this was a different story. Well, of course, it was one, really, I would say, one big action to clear and to clean the Germans. But we had no contact, official, with Nuremberg. Nuremberg was Nuremberg. And we have been-- Dachau was Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: So maybe here, if it would be a problem of big generals, of Gestapo chiefs, and so on, they would be sent to Nuremberg to the international court. Because here, we had a general who was chief justice in the court and-- Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: American officers--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the chief--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the court.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Who was the chief justice?

speaker: interviewee
text: A general, American general.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they changed. But in Nuremberg, as you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were different. They were Englishmen and French and Russian and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: United Nation.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. The, the court itself was part of Dachau. So the prisoners were just brought up from the cells.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't have to actually travel.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the, in the perimeter of, of the big camp. There was a court.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there wasn't a necessity to transport them or anything like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a makeshift courtroom, or was it a, a proper courtroom? Or was it just a room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a big, I would say, a big barrack, changed a little be adjusted to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: To provide.

speaker: interviewee
text: Court. And this is-- well, it was quite different. And sometime, you had two or five--

speaker: interviewer
text: Defendant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Defendant, and sometime you have 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- were, were the trials open to the media? Were journalists allowed to come in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. Yes. There was nothing secret.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was nothing secret about it. It was all totally open?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, you see, the family could come to listen to this and press. But at this time, you know, there was not-- if the press was attending, they came to the Nuremberg trial. You had a doctor trial. You had the businessman trial with Krupp and so on. You had the different trials there, much more interesting and much more, I would say, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] famous defendants.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- during that, during that period of your, of your work with the war crimes, what impressed you the most? What sort of, what memory remains with you of that particular period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you thought was positive or good about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was concerns of war criminals. Well, I say-- I, I saw, I understood, that if you are or deeply involved in a certain ideology or you are opportunist, you can arrive to a stage like they arrived-- the SS in the concentration camp or in Gestapo. They saved themselves for going to the front here, because they have done a good job here. And they believed, for example, exterminating the Jews and the gypsies and the Poles and the Russians, that they are going, doing a reasonably good job for the German nation. They are building up the future of the big Germany. You know, this is a question which I personally say everybody who is looking on their life through their ideology is half blind. You must understand to be objective. Everything got both sides. Any medal, as we say in Poland, a medal got two sides. This is one thing. And one concern-- my life, you know, my surviving of the concentration camp, it was a tremendous lesson for me for good and of bad. There was a concentration of human feelings in the, in the camp. You had beautiful, helping people like my friend, Leon Stein. And you had people who, even Jews, who tried to survive killing others. There was-- I know a couple of cases where a Jew who wanted to survive by force was put before the court and sent to prison after the war for his cruelty against the Jews. This is one thing. The second thing-- I am always happy. I am-- I don't worry about anything in my life. Because I say, well, each day of my life is a gift. I supposed not to live today. I supposed not to talk to you today. I supposed to be piece of-- a bundle of ashes somewhere in the river in Poland or in somewhere there. And the third things-- it's nice to have something, a car, a house, a picture, a TV, a son. But for me, this is not important. For me, it's important to know that I got in my drawer in the kitchen a piece of bread, that if I am hungry during the night, I can go up to the kitchen. And I will have my piece of bread. And I am happy. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The last thing I'd like to just ask you before we ask your wife to just come and have a picture with you, you were awarded various medals after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit about those awards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I received-- from the Polish government, I received for bravery, the Cross of Bravery for my intelligence work. It was a rather risky business, you know? Then I received the Gold Cross of Merit for my war criminal interrogation, and especially for making available to the Polish Justice Department a couple of war criminals. And then recently, I received here in South Africa, I was accorded this Cross of Auschwitz, you know, due to the my involvement in this underground in Auschwitz a little bit and escaping people, helping people to escape. Well, and I say, well, I received because I survived. You know, it's normal, you know. But generally, in the war, the biggest crosses and medals receive people after dead. But this was something special. You see, alive [LAUGHS] I received something.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's true, indeed. And after, after you left the war crimes, when you decided-- you, you said you felt that war crimes were-- were-- being involved in the war crimes were giving you problems with your personality. What did you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came back to Poland. And I started to be a journalist. I was involved in a specialty in motorization. I was specialized in cars and motorbikes and things like that, speedway, and so on. And I was writing about this, which gave me a chance not to be a journalist in this time of socialistic Poland, to be expressing myself on political matters-- and sport, afterwards, you know, a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was writing. I was working for TV as freelancer, of course. I was not in the TV department of Polish television. But I was a freelancer. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as I was a professional, I would say, in motor sport, so if it was a motor sport, they asked me to give comments. It was a, I don't know,a big rally or a racing or things like that, and so on-- the same with radio and other magazines.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and when did you come to South Africa?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came here in 1971 because my brother was here. He was sent by his company. He was working for Renault in France. He was sent to organize after-sales service in South Africa for the factory. And he enjoyed here very much. But his wife was very sick on cancer.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he asked me to come for couple of time-- a couple of weeks here to help a little at home and this. And I, as I was very close to my sister-in-law, you know, and she under-- he understood that when I came here, this is a part of her past, of her Warsaw, where she was from, from her country. And then when I arrived here. And then I was, after two or three months, I was offered a job-- not too bad. And I started, and well, I was working here. And then I was offered from one job to another, to Hewlett I came. And then from Hewlett, I was offered a job, I would say, provincial or governmental, if you wondered. This, I was a public relation officer for [INAUDIBLE] here, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I was working till I was 69. I decided enough is enough, now enjoy a little bit of a beautiful surrounding. We sold our house in Westville and moved in the mountains here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's where you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the forest, where I am now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. That's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: I still enjoy my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful. I think we'll just close off there, and then we'll ask--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, may I introduce my wife, Mary. And I would say that I can say that I met Mary thanks to Auschwitz. It's funny. Because I was invited to Johannesburg, to the Polish Association, to read some of my memoirs from the concentration camp time, from the war criminals time. And you know how it is. I was reading my memoir, my short stories. And there was a person sitting in the audience, I would say reading from my lips what I say. And I was very touched with somebody so much interested in this, what I am telling, what I am reading. There was a break. And on, in this big room was about, I would say, maybe 200 people, so on. Among them was a-- one of the Jesuit father who was my chief of education in my college, in the college where I was in Poland before the war. And he called me and introduced her to me-- and telling me that, you know, when I was in Lusaka, I was close friends to their family so met each other. So and so we met. So the story started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So a story started, you see. When couple of years later-- it was a couple of years-- he realized that we are closer to each other. He called me one day and said, Kaz, I want to warn you. She is a very intelligent person, but she could be a witch. [LAUGHTER] So don't blame me. Well, since then-- I, so I, I live with a witch you know. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He took the risk. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, so it is. Anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mary.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Would, would you like to tell us, maybe give us a little message to your children, your grandchildren, what you're living with, your experiences and listening to the stories and that sort of thing about the-- Kaz's experiences in Auschwitz and the war crimes, how it affected your life? How you feel, what-- what the importance is of knowing about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Kaz doesn't really like talking about it. But he has obviously talked to me about it-- purely, probably, trying to make me understand why he reacts at certain times the way he does, so that I can read him. It has certainly changed his life. It has certainly made him quite a bit different than the run-of-the-mill of men, which is probably-- it's probably done the same to, to a number of, of other people who have gone through the same experiences. The message-- we cannot change the world. We cannot change the, the, the makeup of people. But I think we can do a lot about ourselves and about teaching our children of inhumanity that can be, that can be given or-- not given. It's the wrong word-- you know, [? can you ?] experienced by other people, that we must never, ever repeat this type of thing. We must never, ever be racialist and be intolerant of anything-- nationality, religion, race.

speaker: interviewer
text: Disability, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: Disabilities, all that. Tolerance, I think, is something this world requires.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the message for your children and grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kaz, your last words and your last message?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last message? Be happy. Approach each day, every day, with a smile. Enjoy your life-- not the material life, but the freedom in which are you living. The freedom-- I don't say, I don't say freedom of speech, freedom of expression-- the freedom that you can walk out. You are not scared that somebody will take you or kill you because you are dark or you are white, because you got curly hairs or you got white, long hairs. Not important. Enjoy and be friend to everybody and to everything. Be friends to your dog. Be friends to your children. Be friend to your mother, mother-in-law, grandparents. Be friendly. When you-- I would say, if you give your hand, you stretch your hand to somebody, always some people will take your hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a lovely message. Thank you very much, indeed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Kaz, you can talk about them now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, here they are, the four decoration which I received. The first one-- of course, they are all in miniatures, you know? They are not in full size. In full size, I got only one here. But this is a Cross for Bravery, the first one. The second one is a Gold Cross of Merit. The third one is a Auschwitz Cross. And the fourth one is a Victory Medal. Well, this is a miniature. And this in a natural big size, the Auschwitz Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kaz, can you just tell us what the difference is?

words.text: just tell us what the difference is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't carry them at all generally, only for, I would say, very special military occasion or, sorry to say, for military funerals. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a Polish Eagle from a military cap. Well, this is a picture of myself, well, in 1922, in November-- so many, many years ago from such a pretty baby, such a awful old man now.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is me and my brother. I was, at this time, about eight. And he was six, two years younger-- Andre, or Andrew, or as we call in Polish, Anje.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's you on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. He's on the right. He was blonde, and I was dark. And he was curly, very much with long curls here, looking like a girl. And many times, somebody stopped our nanny with-- or my mother-- with us and told, what a beautiful daughter you got. And he was cross like hell and jump and say, I am a boy! [LAUGHS] So it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, this is a photo of my Jesuit college, a photo of my class done just before the war. It was done in June, '39. And the war started in September, '39. So it is the last photograph here. I am here [? or ?] in the middle. And this is the Jesuit father who introduced me to my wife here in South Africa-- that one. He passed away already, a couple of years ago-- A very kind, a very intelligent chap, like, generally, the Jesuit are. [? MARIA WOLFF-ZDZNIECKI: ?] [INAUDIBLE] [INTERPOSING VOICES] Well, and this here, you got me in my army uniform just after the war in Germany during the winter. And well, so it was a war time almost finished. After the war, this is it. Now you can see my son, his wife, and my three grandchildren in Poland. You got in the middle on top is Barbara, student of law in the University of Krakow. The boy is Marek, doing this year, '96, matric. And the youngest, Marta-- what she will be, let's wait and see. And here--
